1.exposition: The chapter breaks over the reader like daybreak after the long night of Romans 7. The despairing cry, “Wretched man that I am! Who shall deliver me?” is answered at a stroke: there is therefore now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus. What follows is Paul’s fullest teaching on the Holy Spirit — the word “Spirit,” scarcely heard in chapters 6–7, now sounds again and again. The argument runs in a single arc: the law could not justify or give life, weakened as it was through the flesh; so God did what the law could not — he sent his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh and condemned sin in the flesh, that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us who walk by the Spirit. Two humanities are then set side by side — the mind of the flesh, which is death, and the mind of the Spirit, which is life and peace (v.6). And the decisive mark of belonging is named: the Spirit of God indwelling, the pledge that the same power which raised Jesus will one day give life to our mortal bodies.
1.original_language: Everything turns on the word nomos (“law”). In the phrase “the law of the Spirit of life” and “the law of sin and death” (v.2), nomos is best taken not as two Mosaic codes but as two ruling powers or governing principles — the reign of the life-giving Spirit against the reign of sin. Sarx (“flesh”) throughout denotes not the body as such but human nature in its fallenness and self-reliance; the phronēma tēs sarkos (v.6, “the mind-set of the flesh”) is its whole bent and orientation. In v.3 two phrases are weighed with care: homoiōmati sarkos hamartias (“in the likeness of sinful flesh”) guards both the reality of the incarnation and the sinlessness of the Son, while peri hamartias (“for sin”) is the Greek Old Testament’s standing term for the sin-offering.
1.historical_context: The section is the pneumatological centre of the letter, and it answers a cry raised in the previous chapter (7:24). The repeated “in Christ Jesus” marks out the sphere in which the Spirit works: union with Christ is the location of the new-covenant life the law could never produce. For readers formed by the synagogue, the admission that the law was “weakened through the flesh” was startling — not that the law was evil, but that it was powerless to remedy the corruption of those to whom it was given. What Sinai could only command, the indwelling Spirit now supplies.
1.christ: Here stands Paul’s clearest word on the incarnation and the cross in the whole chapter. God sent “his own Son” — not an angel or a prophet, but the Son who shares the Father’s own identity — in real human flesh, yet without sin. And in that flesh God “condemned sin” (v.3): at the cross the judgment due to sin was executed upon the sin-bearer, so that no condemnation remains for those he represents. The resurrection completes the movement — the Spirit “of him who raised Jesus from the dead” now dwells in mortal bodies (v.11), so that the believer’s future rising is bound by one Spirit and one power to the rising of Christ himself.

verses.note: The keynote of the chapter, struck in a single phrase: no condemnation. reformed Gill hears the apostle, having laid bare the inward war of the previous chapter, now turning to “the solid ground and foundation” of the saints’ peace — justification, adoption, the purposes of God, and “the everlasting and unchangeable love of God in Christ.” grammatical-historical Barnes weighs the triumphant “therefore”: the adverse verdict is not suspended but entered and irreversible, and its sphere is union — “to them which are in Christ Jesus.” The walk after the Spirit is the mark of the freed, not the ground of their freedom.

The reason for the acquittal: the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus has set me free from the law of sin and death. reformed Gill grants the terms are “of difficult interpretation,” but the dominant sense reads “law” as a governing power — the new principle of life imparted by the Spirit overthrowing the ruling power of indwelling sin. grammatical-historical Barnes marks the two dominions in conflict, the Spirit’s life prevailing where “the Spirit of the Lord is,” for “there is liberty” (2 Corinthians 3:17).

What the law could not do — being weak through the flesh — God did. eastern Theodoret savours the exactness of the phrase: Paul “says not in the likeness of flesh, but in the likeness of sinful flesh, for he received actually the human nature, but human sin he received not.” reformed Calvin insists the clause speaks of free justification — the Son sent “for sin” (as a sin-offering) so that God “condemned sin in the flesh,” executing upon Christ the judgment sin deserved. Incarnation and atonement are named in one breath.

The purpose of the cross: that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us. reformed Gill reads the fulfilment first by imputation — Christ, as his people’s representative, perfectly kept the law, and his obedience is reckoned to them, so its whole demand is met “in us” (in our Head) — and then evidenced by the Spirit-led walk. The law is not set aside but honoured (Romans 3:31): fulfilled for us by Christ, and increasingly worked out in us by the Spirit.

Two humanities, marked by what they “mind”: those who are after the flesh set their minds on the things of the flesh. reformed Gill draws a careful line — to be “after the flesh” is not merely to have flesh (the best of the saints have it), but to be governed by it, to have it as the ruling principle. The affections follow the master: “where your treasure is, there will your heart be also” (Matthew 6:21).

The two mind-sets issue in two ends: the mind of the flesh is death, but the mind of the Spirit is life and peace. reformed Gill notes the phrase reaches even to “the wisdom of the flesh” — the best and highest part of corrupt man — and finds it still death; the spiritual mind, by contrast, is life (communion with God now, and eternal life) and peace (reconciliation and a quieted conscience).

Why the fleshly mind is death: it is hostile to God; it does not submit to God’s law — indeed it cannot. reformed Gill presses the depth — the carnal mind “is not only an enemy, but enmity itself,” set against God’s being, his law, and his gospel — and the depth of the inability: “neither indeed can be.” The natural man “cannot know” the things of the Spirit (1 Corinthians 2:14); the corruption runs so deep that it drives the sinner to grace for a new heart.

The verdict on the unrenewed: those who are in the flesh cannot please God. grammatical-historical Barnes identifies them as “unrenewed sinners,” following the desires of the flesh and not led by the Spirit; reformed Gill adds that these are the same as those “after the flesh.” Being enmity against God and unable to submit (v.7), their very best works cannot please him — for “without faith it is impossible to please him” (Hebrews 11:6).

The decisive turn to the believer: you are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if in fact the Spirit of God dwells in you. grammatical-historical Barnes marks that Christians are “the opposite of those described.” Then the searching test: “if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, he does not belong to him.” The indwelling Spirit — not profession, not outward religion — is the mark of belonging; and the same Spirit is called both “of God” and “of Christ,” an unforced witness to the deity of both.

The paradox of the indwelt life: if Christ is in you, the body is dead because of sin, but the Spirit is life because of righteousness. reformed Gill notes that Christ is “formed in the hearts” of his people by the Spirit in regeneration — “the Father reveals him not only to them, but in them.” The body remains mortal, appointed to die because of sin; but the renewed spirit is alive, and alive precisely “because of righteousness” — the imputed righteousness of Christ the ground of its life.

The mortal body’s certain hope: he who raised Christ from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through his Spirit who dwells in you. reformed Gill insists this is not the ongoing work of sanctification but “the future quickening of the mortal body — the resurrection,” for it is the bodies, not the souls, that are said to be made alive. The indwelling Spirit is the pledge: the very power that raised Christ, the firstfruits, will raise those who are his (1 Corinthians 15:23).

witnesses.point: “No condemnation” rests on the solid ground of justification, adoption, and the unchangeable love of God in Christ — union with Christ is the ground of freedom, and the Spirit-led walk only its mark.
The triumphant “therefore” enters an irreversible verdict; the indwelling Spirit is the decisive mark distinguishing those in the Spirit from those in the flesh, who cannot please God.
Paul writes “likeness of sinful flesh,” not merely “likeness of flesh”: the Son truly took the human nature, but not human sin.
God, sending his Son “for sin” as a sin-offering, condemned sin in Christ’s flesh — free justification accomplished where the law was powerless.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: Reads “the law of the Spirit of life” as the Holy Spirit himself, the life-giving power now ruling the believer, and takes the flesh–Spirit antithesis of vv.5–8 as the clash of two powers or eras; “no condemnation” is the banner text over the whole chapter.

1.application: The Christian life does not move toward “no condemnation”; it flows from it. To believe v.1 is to stop treating God’s verdict as pending, and to let the Spirit who secured it now shape the mind, the walk, and the hope of the body’s rising.

12.exposition: From status Paul turns to family. Because the Spirit indwells, the believer is a debtor — but not to the flesh, which has earned nothing but death. The way of life is to put to death the deeds of the body by the Spirit; and all who are so led by the Spirit of God are sons of God. Then the note deepens from law to intimacy: the believer has not received a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry, “Abba! Father!” The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God — and children are heirs, heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ, if indeed we suffer with him so as to be glorified with him. Sonship is the standing; mortification the practice; and the inheritance is reached along the same road Christ walked, from cross to crown.
12.original_language: The section’s heart is the cry Abba ho patēr (v.15) — the Aramaic Abba followed by its Greek rendering ho patēr, the very words Jesus prayed in Gethsemane (Mark 14:36; cf. Galatians 4:6). Huiothesia (“adoption,” literally “placing as a son”) is a term from Roman law, not the Old Testament, denoting the conferral of full legal sonship and inheritance. Paul sets pneuma douleias (“a spirit of slavery”) against pneuma huiothesias (“the Spirit of adoption”), and in v.16 uses symmartyrei — the Spirit bears witness together with our spirit, a joint testimony.
12.historical_context: Roman adoptio was a recognized legal act by which a man could take an heir — even a grown man from outside the family — and confer on him his name, his property, and full standing as a son, the old debts and status cancelled. Paul reaches for the highest form of belonging his culture knew to describe the believer’s new place before God. And Abba was the ordinary, intimate word a child used of a father; that it survives untranslated on the lips of Greek-speaking churches shows how deeply Jesus’ own manner of address had entered Christian prayer.
12.christ: When the Spirit puts “Abba, Father” on the believer’s lips, he is lending them the Son’s own prayer — drawing them into the relationship Jesus has with the Father by nature, which they now share by grace. So too the inheritance: believers are fellow heirs with Christ, sharing what belongs to the eternal Son, who is “the firstborn among many brothers” (v.29). And the condition of the heirship — “if indeed we suffer with him” — is not a doubt but the pattern: the same union that joins us to the crucified Christ joins us to the glorified Christ, cross and resurrection held in one bond.

verses.note: The practical turn: we are debtors — not to the flesh, to live according to the flesh. eastern Chrysostom admires the striking form of the exhortation: Paul does not merely say “we ought not to live after the flesh,” but “we are debtors” — and by saying we are debtors “not to the flesh,” he quietly indicates that we are debtors to the Spirit. What God has done for us is “not matter of debt, but of mere grace,” yet grace lays on us the sweetest obligation.

The two ways and their ends: if you live according to the flesh you will die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the deeds of the body, you will live. reformed Gill notes that although flesh remains in the believer it no longer has dominion; the killing of sin is a duty (“ye… do mortify”) yet done “through the Spirit,” never by mere resolve — “put to death therefore what is earthly in you” (Colossians 3:5). Life is not earned by the war on sin, but evidenced and maintained in it.

The mark of sonship: as many as are led by the Spirit of God, these are sons of God. reformed Gill describes the leading — the Spirit draws his people “from sin, and from a dependence on their own righteousness,” to Christ, in the paths of faith and holiness. It is efficacious grace, and yet gentle, as a child is taught to walk: powerful, tender, and always for their good. Those so led are known to be sons.

Not slavery but sonship: you did not receive the spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but the Spirit of adoption. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the term huiothesia and that both Jew and Gentile receive this adoption with all its privileges, crying “Abba, Father” (cf. Galatians 4:6). wesleyan-arminian Wesley hears in “cry” a vehement speaking “with desire, confidence, constancy,” the Aramaic and Greek together voicing “the joint cry” of Jew and Gentile. eastern Theodoret adds that the “spirit of bondage” is not the Holy Spirit himself but the law’s regime of fear now left behind.

The inner assurance: the Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God. grammatical-historical Barnes argues the Holy Spirit is meant — it is of the Spirit that Paul is treating — and that his witness joins with “our spirit,” the renewed conscience, in a double testimony. The Spirit witnesses through his fruits and his enabling of the filial cry; the guard against presumption is that this witness is confirmed by the marks of grace, not a bare impulse.

Sons, therefore heirs: heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ, if indeed we suffer with him in order that we may also be glorified with him. reformed Gill marks the surpassing dignity — the believer is heir not merely of created blessings but “of God himself,” his very portion, and co-heir with Christ of all that is his. The suffering “with him” is not the meritorious cause of glory but its appointed road: “if we endure, we shall also reign with him” (2 Timothy 2:12).

witnesses.point: Paul says “we are debtors not to the flesh” — a striking form that shows salvation is all of grace, yet lays on believers the obligation of the Spirit, to whom the true debt is owed.
“Adoption” (huiothesia) is received by Jew and Gentile alike, with the filial cry “Abba, Father” — the Aramaic and Greek together, echoing Galatians 4:6.
“Cry” denotes a vehement speaking with desire, confidence, and constancy; the double name “Abba, Father” voices the joint cry of Jewish and Gentile believers.
The believer is heir of God himself and joint-heir with Christ; suffering with him is not the cause of glory but the appointed path to it.

external.name: N.T. Wright
external.point: Hears a new-exodus pattern beneath the sonship language: as Israel was redeemed, led by God through the wilderness, named his son, and given an inheritance, so the Spirit now leads God’s children as sons and heirs — the promised inheritance enlarged from a land to the whole renewed creation.

12.application: The Spirit does not first make us try harder; he first makes us cry “Father.” Assurance of sonship (v.16) is meant to precede and fuel the mortifying of sin (v.13) — sons put sin to death, not to become children, but because they already are.

18.exposition: The mention of suffering (v.17) opens onto a cosmic horizon. Paul “reckons” — a deliberate calculation of faith — that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory to be revealed in us. Then the lens widens past the believer to the whole created order: the creation waits “on tiptoe” for the revealing of the sons of God, for it was subjected to futility — not by its own will, but by God, and “in hope.” One day the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay into the freedom of the glory of God’s children. Meanwhile the whole creation groans together in the pains of childbirth — and we ourselves, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we await the redemption of our bodies. This is salvation held in hope: what is seen is not hoped for, so we wait for it with patience. The groan is not despair; it is a labour pang, and labour ends in birth.
18.original_language: The creation’s posture in v.19 is apokaradokia — a vivid word picturing a watcher craning the neck, straining forward with outstretched head. It was subjected to mataiotēs (v.20, “futility, emptiness, frustration”), the same barren purposelessness Ecclesiastes laments. In v.22 Paul coins a double compound: the creation systenazei kai synōdinei — “groans together and travails together,” the -ōdin- root naming specifically the pangs of childbirth. And the Spirit already given is the aparchē (v.23, “firstfruits”) — the first, representative installment of a harvest whose fullness is the apolytrōsis tou sōmatos, the redemption of the body.
18.historical_context: That the material world shares both in humanity’s fall and in its final redemption is deeply Jewish. The ground was cursed for Adam’s sake (Genesis 3:17), and the prophets looked for new heavens and a new earth (Isaiah 65:17) in which even the predatory order is healed (Isaiah 11:6-9). The “redemption of our bodies” (v.23) is no escape of the soul from the body but the body’s transformation, the resurrection hope Paul unfolds in 1 Corinthians 15:42-44. The labour metaphor frames the present as transition, not termination.
18.christ: The redemption of the body is a christological endpoint: Christ’s resurrection is the prototype, and the believer’s bodily rising its extension — the body made like “his glorious body” (Philippians 3:21). The “revealing of the sons of God” (v.19) is the public unveiling of what union with Christ has already accomplished in secret. And the creation’s liberation shows the cosmic reach of his work: what was lost at the fall is restored not for souls only but for the whole order, through the one who is “the firstborn of all creation” and “the firstborn from the dead” (Colossians 1:15-18).

verses.note: The calculation of faith: the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory to be revealed in us. reformed Gill reads “this present time” as the whole span of the saints’ earthly life, in which grace and strength answerable to their trials are given. Paul does not deny the weight of the suffering; he weighs it, and finds it as nothing beside “a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory” (2 Corinthians 4:17) — a glory “revealed in us,” not merely shown to us.

The creation on tiptoe: the earnest expectation of the creation waits for the revealing of the sons of God. Here the witnesses divide. reformed Gill, taking “the creature” of the Gentile world, reads its longing as the nations’ yearning for gospel light; grammatical-historical Barnes, with most, takes it of the whole irrational creation, personified and craning forward for the day of redemption. Either way, the creation’s destiny is bound to the manifestation of the sons.

Subjected, but in hope: the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of him who subjected it, in hope. grammatical-historical Barnes reads the natural creation bound over to frailty and decay by God’s own sentence after the fall (Genesis 3:17-18) — an innocent sufferer for man’s sin, “not willingly,” yet not without hope. reformed Gill, on his reading, hears the emptiness of the Gentiles’ idolatry. The subjection is real, but it is not the last word.

The hope named: the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to decay and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God. grammatical-historical Barnes sees the natural world freed from corruption, death, and the curse, brought to share the liberty and glory of the redeemed — the new heavens and new earth (2 Peter 3:13; Revelation 21:1). The world’s deliverance is knit to the children’s: when they are glorified, it is set free.

The universal groan: the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now. grammatical-historical Barnes takes the image at face value — a creation not dying but in travail, its pangs the labour that precedes a birth. The groaning that has run on “until now,” from the fall to the present hour, is therefore a sign of hope, not of despair: what travails is about to bring forth the new creation.

And believers groan too: we ourselves, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we wait for adoption, the redemption of our bodies. grammatical-historical Barnes explains the harvest figure — the “firstfruits” are the first portion, the present graces of the Spirit, the earnest of the full harvest to come. Already adopted (v.15), the believer still awaits the adoption’s consummation in the body’s redemption (Philippians 3:21); the very taste of the Spirit deepens the longing.

Saved in the mode of hope: in this hope we were saved; but hope that is seen is not hope, for who hopes for what he sees? reformed Gill notes the saints are already saved in God’s purpose, the covenant, and Christ’s purchase, yet the full enjoyment is future, held now in hope. And hope, by its nature, reaches toward the unseen: what is already in hand is not the object of hope.

Hope’s fruit is patience: if we hope for what we do not see, we wait for it with patience. grammatical-historical Barnes observes the principle at work everywhere — where there is strong desire joined to a real expectation of obtaining (which is exactly what true hope is), we can wait with patience. So the unseen glory steadies the soul through the present groaning.

witnesses.point: Present sufferings, though real, are outweighed beyond comparison by the coming glory; salvation is already possessed in God’s purpose and Christ’s purchase, yet held now in hope of what is unseen.

Takes “the creation” as the whole natural order, personified — subjected to decay by God’s sentence after the fall, yet in hope, groaning as in birth-pangs toward its liberation with the children of God.
The afflictions of the faithful are the very means by which they are conformed to the image of Christ, and so no cause for grief to those who consent to bear his image.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: Argues on close exegesis that “the creation” (ktisis) in vv.19–22 means the whole sub-human created order — neither humanity nor angels — personified as sharing humanity’s fall and awaiting, with it, the liberation that the revealing of God’s children will bring.

18.application: The difference between despair and hope is often only the difference between a death-rattle and a birth-pang. Paul teaches the sufferer to reinterpret the groan (v.22): not the sound of the world ending, but of a new one being born.

26.exposition: Into the weakness of the groaning comes help. The Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know what to pray for as we ought — but the Spirit himself intercedes “with groanings too deep for words,” and God who searches hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, whose intercession is always “according to the will of God.” On this footing rests the great assurance: God works all things together for good to those who love him, who are called according to his purpose. And the purpose is then unfolded backward and forward in a single golden chain: those God foreknew he predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that the Son might be “the firstborn among many brothers”; and those he predestined, he called; those he called, he justified; those he justified, he glorified. The chain reaches from eternity past to glory, and it does not lose a single link along the way.
26.original_language: Verses 29–30 are five aorist verbs bolted end to end — the “golden chain.” Proegnō (“foreknew”), proōrisen (“predestined,” marked out beforehand), ekalesen (“called”), edikaiōsen (“justified”), edoxasen (“glorified”). The last is the striking one: the glorification still future is put in the past tense — a proleptic aorist — because in God’s purpose it is as good as done. The debated word is proginōskō (“foreknow”): in Paul it regularly carries the Hebraic sense of relational, elective knowing (cf. Romans 11:2; 1 Corinthians 8:3), to “set one’s regard upon” beforehand, though many read it as prior knowledge of what a person will do. The goal is symmorphous tēs eikonos — “conformed to the image” — of the Son.
26.historical_context: This “golden chain” has stood at the centre of the Reformed and Arminian debate for centuries, and the ancient and modern witnesses genuinely divide over it (see the notes on vv.29–30). The question is whether “foreknew” names God’s prior love and choice, or his foresight of who would believe — and whether the identical “whom… them” of each link means the same, unlosable company runs from foreknowledge to glory, or whether Paul describes the God-appointed method without fixing the number at each stage. The Spirit’s “groanings too deep for words” (v.26) are best read not as tongues but as the Spirit’s own participation in the believer’s prayer, carrying what cannot be said.
26.christ: The end of predestination is a person: conformity to the image of God’s Son. God’s purpose was never merely forensic (justification) but transformative — that the redeemed should be made like Christ, in holiness now and glory hereafter. And Christ is set as “the firstborn among many brothers”: not a solitary Son but the elder brother of a whole family, the prototype of the new humanity whose resurrection glory his people will come to share. To be saved, in the end, is to be made like the Son and gathered into his family.

verses.note: Help in the weakness of prayer: the Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know what to pray for as we ought, but the Spirit himself intercedes with groanings too deep for words. reformed Gill notes that the same Spirit who indwells, leads, and witnesses to our adoption also bears our infirmities — and weakness in prayer is one of them. The intercession is not made apart from us but within us: the Spirit excites and shapes the wordless groanings of the praying heart.

And the groan is understood: he who searches hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for the saints according to the will of God. grammatical-historical Barnes notes that to search the heart is God’s incommunicable attribute (Jeremiah 17:10); he reads the desires the Spirit stirs, inarticulate though they are. Because that intercession is always “according to the will of God,” the believer’s Spirit-shaped prayers are certain to be heard — “if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us” (1 John 5:14).

The comprehensive assurance: we know that God works all things together for good to those who love him, who are called according to his purpose. eastern Chrysostom notes that Paul raised this for believers in danger — teaching them “not to choose just what they may think themselves to be useful, but what the Spirit may suggest,” leaving all to the God who works all for good. reformed Gill adds that the “good” is the believer’s true, spiritual, and eternal good — conformity to Christ and final glory — not always present ease.

The chain begins, and the great debate with it: those he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son. reformed Gill argues “foreknew” cannot mean bare prescience — for in that sense God foreknows all, and then all would be predestined and glorified; nor foresight of faith or good works, for that would make something in man, not God’s good pleasure, the cause. It means to forelove, to set electing love on a people; so reformed Calvin: “not a bare prescience… but the adoption by which he had always distinguished his children.” Others read it otherwise. wesleyan-arminian Wesley makes those foreknown simply those found conformable to the Son. grammatical-historical Ellicott urges that foreknowledge cannot cancel free will — “a man does not choose an action because it is foreknown, but it is foreknown because he will choose it.” And eastern Theodoret insists foreknowledge is not causal at all: “foreknowledge made them not such as they are, but God… foresaw from of old all that would be.”

The chain completed: those he predestined he also called; those he called he also justified; those he justified he also glorified. reformed Gill sets predestination at the head as the source of every following blessing, each link drawing the next unbroken, so that the already-past “glorified” speaks of a thing so sure it is reckoned done — “of all he has given me I should lose nothing” (John 6:39). wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the same words as the divine method rather than a fixed tally: Paul “does not affirm… that precisely the same number… are called, justified, and glorified,” nor deny that one may fall away between his calling and his glory (Romans 11:22). The links are agreed; whether the company at each is identical is exactly the point in dispute.

witnesses.point: “Foreknew” means foreloved, not bare prescience or foreseen faith — else all would be glorified; predestination is the source of the whole chain, and the proleptic “glorified” makes the elect’s glory as good as done.

The chain describes God’s method of leading believers to heaven, not a fixed number at each stage; Paul does not deny that one may fall away between calling and glory, nor that many are called who are never justified.
Foreknowledge does not interfere with free will — a thing is foreknown because a person will choose it, not chosen because foreknown; predestination must not be read to exclude free will.

God’s foreknowledge is not the cause of what it foresees: as predicting a runaway horse’s fall does not push it over the cliff, so God foreknew the disposition he would then predestine, call, justify, and glorify.

external.name: Thomas R. Schreiner
external.point: Defends the Reformed reading: proginōskō carries the Old Testament sense of covenantal, elective knowing — to “forelove” — rather than foresight of faith; and because each verb governs the same group, the chain is unbroken and the calling of v.30 is effectual, securing the believer’s final glory.

26.application: The chain is not first a diagram to argue over but an anchor to hold. The same God who predestined, called, justified, and will glorify is the God who hears the Spirit’s wordless intercession (v.26) when you cannot find a prayer — and works even this hour’s trouble toward your good (v.28).

31.exposition: Now the argument becomes a hymn. What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? The blessings of the golden chain are a complete set, wanting nothing; against the God who gives them no adversary can stand. The proof is the cross: he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all will surely, with him, give us all things — the greater gift guaranteeing every lesser one. Then two courtroom challenges, each answered before it is finished: who shall bring a charge against God’s elect? It is God who justifies; who is to condemn? Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised, who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us. Last, the challenge of separation: who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Not tribulation, nor sword, nor the martyr’s lot of Psalm 44; in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us. Paul ends persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor height nor depth nor anything else in all creation, can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord. The chapter that began with no condemnation ends with no separation; the believer is held at both ends.
31.original_language: The peroration is built of legal and military words. In v.33 egkalesei is a court term — “to bring a charge, to lay an accusation”; in v.34 ho katakrinōn is the one who would pass the sentence of condemnation. Against both stands ho entynchanōn — Christ “who intercedes,” the Advocate at court. The chapter’s coined climax is hypernikōmen (v.37), a rare and late compound found nowhere else in the New Testament: not merely nikaō, “we conquer,” but “we super-conquer,” we win a surpassing, overwhelming victory — and win it “through him who loved us.”
31.historical_context: The passage is shaped as a courtroom scene giving way to a triumph-song. The rhetorical questions — who accuses? who condemns? who separates? — are the moves of a defence that has already won. The citation in v.36 is Psalm 44:22, a lament of the righteous suffering community; Paul takes the psalmist’s complaint and folds it inside his confidence — the very experience of being “sheep for the slaughter” now falls within “more than conquerors.” The catalogue of vv.38–39 — angels, rulers, powers, height, depth — reads like a roster of the cosmic forces the ancient world believed governed human fate, each named only to be dismissed.
31.christ: The centre of the passage is the most compressed christological creed in Romans: Christ Jesus who died — more than that, was raised — who is at the right hand of God — who indeed is interceding for us. Four saving realities stand in one sentence. His death paid the penalty; his resurrection proved the payment accepted, our justification secured (Romans 4:25); his exaltation seats him in authority; his intercession pleads that finished work continually — “he is able to save to the uttermost… since he always lives to make intercession” (Hebrews 7:25). And the whole rests on v.32: the God who did not spare his own Son will withhold nothing his people need. Against a died-risen-reigning-interceding Christ, no charge and no condemnation can hold.

verses.note: The triumphant question: if God is for us, who can be against us? reformed Gill notes that “these things” are the blessings just rehearsed — foreknowledge, predestination, calling, justification, glorification — “a complete set of blessings, wanting nothing.” With such a God on his side, the believer need not fear any adversary; many are against him, but none can prevail against the one whom the Almighty defends. The “if” is not a doubt but a settled fact.

The argument from the greatest gift: he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things? reformed Gill finds it amazing that God should not spare “his own Son” — his proper Son, of the same nature, equal to himself, the sinless Son of his love — when he spared not the angels that sinned. If God has given the greatest, he will not withhold the lesser; every needful thing is secured “with him” (cf. 1 Corinthians 3:21-23).

The first challenge: who shall bring a charge against God’s elect? It is God who justifies. reformed Gill notes the elect are a people God loved and chose in Christ before the foundation of the world; chargeable in themselves with real sin, yet no charge can stand — for the highest Judge himself has acquitted them. The accuser is overruled by the very court before whom the charge would be laid (Revelation 12:10).

The second challenge, and the fourfold answer: who is to condemn? Christ Jesus died, was raised, is at God’s right hand, and intercedes for us. grammatical-historical Robertson: it would take a bold accuser to face God with charges when “we have an Advocate” (1 John 2:1) at the right hand of God — “our Advocate paid the debt for our sins with his blood. The score is settled.” reformed Calvin adds that whoever would condemn us now “must bring back Christ himself to death again,” for he rose as the conqueror of death. eastern Theodoret sees the risen Christ seated by the Father, still presenting on our behalf the firstfruits he took from us — death, resurrection, exaltation, and intercession, the four pillars of security.

The last challenge: who shall separate us from the love of Christ? shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword? reformed Gill fixes the crucial point — “the love of Christ” here is Christ’s love to us, not ours to him; and so the bond does not hang on the constancy of our affection. The severest trials are named, the very ones that seem to threaten the union — and not one of them can sever it.

Scripture had said as much: as it is written, “For your sake we are being killed all the day long; we are regarded as sheep to be slaughtered.” reformed Gill cites it from Psalm 44:22: for God’s sake the saints of old, like those of the gospel, were exposed to death all the day long. grammatical-historical Barnes notes Paul quotes it not as originally spoken of Christians but as aptly describing their condition — “I die daily” (1 Corinthians 15:31); suffering is the appointed lot of the godly (2 Timothy 3:12).

Not bare survival but surpassing victory: in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us. grammatical-historical Robertson marks the rare compound hypernikōmen, found only here in the New Testament — “we gain a surpassing victory through the one who loved us.” wesleyan-arminian Wesley presses the “more than”: in all these trials the believer is “not only no losers, but abundant gainers,” the afflictions themselves turned to profit — “thanks be to God, who gives us the victory” (1 Corinthians 15:57).

The sworn persuasion: I am persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers can separate us. reformed Gill notes this is no conjecture and no special private revelation, but the full assurance of faith “founded on the nature of the love of God itself, the security of it in Christ,… eternal predestination and the unalterable purposes of God.” The catalogue ranges over all: death the last enemy, life with its lures, every rank of angel and power, all time — “I know whom I have believed” (2 Timothy 1:12).

The soaring close: nor height, nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord. reformed Gill reads “height and depth” as heaven, earth, and hell, no height of prosperity and no depth of adversity, no power or policy of men or devils — and “any other creature” as anything else in the whole of creation. The universe is surveyed and found powerless; the love is secured “in Christ,” and so the bond holds — “I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish” (John 10:28). The chapter that opened with “no condemnation” closes with “no separation.”

witnesses.point: “The love of Christ” is his love to us, not ours to him, so the bond does not rest on our constancy; Paul’s persuasion is the full assurance of faith, founded on God’s unchangeable love, predestination, and its security in Christ.
In v.34 the believer has an Advocate at God’s right hand whose blood has settled the score; the coined hypernikōmen (v.37) means a surpassing victory won through the one who loved us.
The risen Christ, seated by the Father, still presents our nature on our behalf and intercedes; set against God’s love, all sufferings are things over which we rise superior.
To be “more than conquerors” is to be not merely no losers but abundant gainers by every trial — the afflictions themselves turned to profit through Christ’s love.

external.name: Leon Morris
external.point: Calls Romans 8 the greatest chapter in Scripture and hears in v.34 a fourfold ground of assurance — death, resurrection, exaltation, intercession — while “more than conquerors” means an overwhelming, not a marginal, victory; the security rests on God’s grip on us, not ours on him.

31.application: Read the two ends together: no condemnation and no separation. The same love that acquitted you will not let you go — not because your grip on God is strong, but because his grip on you, fastened at the cross and held by the interceding Son, cannot be broken.

_meta.title: No Condemnation, No Separation
reflection: The chapter is bracketed by two immovable promises — “no condemnation” and “nothing can separate us from the love of God”. What changes in your daily fears when you know you are held at both ends, not by the strength of your grip on God but by the strength of his on you?

At vv.29–30 the witnesses genuinely divide over “foreknew” — whether it names God’s electing love (Gill, Calvin, Schreiner) or his foresight of our response (Wesley, Ellicott, Theodoret). Set the debate aside for a moment: what comfort is Paul actually reaching for when he traces salvation from before the world to final glory?

The Spirit intercedes for us “with groanings too deep for words” when we do not know what to pray. How does it change your prayer life to know that its power does not depend on your finding the right words, but on the Spirit who prays within you according to God’s will (v.27)?
